rescue_reasoning: Although coded as Adaptation, the text focuses on access-oriented development near parks/open space ('increase public access and use') without an explicit adaptation/resilience outcome. Given the green-infrastructure framing in the parent statement and climate_screen=explicit_self, keep it as climate-relevant but better aligned to Nature-Based Solutions (green/open space planning) than Adaptation.
policy_statement: Encourage new development and redevelopment near existing parks and open spaces to increase public access and use.
mechanism_description: Encouraging development and redevelopment near existing parks and open spaces increases public access to and use of green space.
primary_causal_pathway: Land-use pattern → ↑ access to existing green space → ↓ heat exposure/health risk during extreme heat
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in human activity/system: Land-use planning and siting decisions steer housing/jobs/services toward areas near existing parks and open spaces, increasing the number of residents within convenient reach of green space. (2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Vegetation and open space provide localized cooling (shade + evapotranspiration) and can moderate peak temperatures; park areas can also serve as refuges during heat events. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced exposure and sensitivity to extreme heat (and related health impacts), improving community climate resilience.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced population heat exposure by improving access to parks and cooler microclimates, thereby lowering vulnerability during extreme heat events.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Land-use decisions increase access to existing green space, primarily yielding exposure reduction to heat extremes (adaptation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While proximity to parks can support walking/biking and potentially reduce VMT (mitigation/resource efficiency), that outcome is not inherent or assured from “develop near parks” alone; likewise, it does not necessarily add new vegetation/land area to increase carbon sinks (nature-based solutions). The most reliable, direct climate-relevant effect is reduced heat exposure via access to cooler environments.  
3) Edge cases applied: If policies explicitly bundle transit-oriented development, parking reductions, or active-transport mandates, mitigation/resource-efficiency effects could dominate at the policy level; if they include park expansion/tree planting, nature-based benefits could dominate. Those are not part of the canonical mechanism as stated (access to existing green space).  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is “site development near existing parks to increase public access/use” consistently increases access to cooling refuges and thereby supports heat-risk reduction, regardless of local context.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any mitigation co-benefits (e.g., mode shift) or nature-based co-benefits (if parks are enhanced) require policy-specific evidence and are not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “near parks” development results in loss/fragmentation of green space (e.g., encroachment) or induces additional driving/parking demand, adaptation benefits may be reduced and mitigation impacts could be negative; conversely, if coupled with active-mobility networks or transit, mitigation could become the dominant pathway at the policy level.
key_indicators: “Strategy”; “Encourage new development and redevelopment”; “occur near existing parks and open spaces”; “increase their access to and use by the public”
co_benefits: Improved public health and recreation; potential air-quality benefits; community cohesion; potential active transportation benefits (if paired with access improvements)
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented via incentives or zoning overlays in practice, but the statement itself is framed as general guidance (“encourage”) with no specific regulatory, funding, or performance requirements.
